seoTitle: How to Use Google Docs for Group Projects Easily
metaDescription: Learn how to set up Google Docs for team collaboration, using Suggesting mode, comments, @mentions, and version history to manage group work.
intro: Coauthoring a group project doesn't have to mean chaotic overwrites and lost edits. Google Docs offers real-time collaboration tools that let teams share access, propose changes safely, assign tasks, and roll back mistakes. This guide covers setting up group permissions and using the key collaboration features to keep everyone aligned.

steps.heading: Share the document with your team
steps.body: Click 'Share,' add teammates' email addresses, and set their permission to 'Editor' so they can contribute.

steps.heading: Switch to Suggesting mode
steps.body: Change the editing mode toggle in the top right from 'Editing' to 'Suggesting' so proposed changes don't overwrite the master text.

steps.heading: Use comments to give feedback
steps.body: Highlight a sentence and click the comment icon to start a feedback thread tied to that specific text.

steps.heading: Assign tasks with @mentions
steps.body: Type '@' followed by a teammate's name inside a comment to notify them directly and assign a specific task.

steps.heading: Track and restore versions
steps.body: Go to File > Version History to review the color-coded revision timeline and restore an earlier version if something goes wrong.

faq.question: Can multiple people edit the document at the same time?
faq.answer: Yes, Google Docs supports real-time simultaneous editing, showing each collaborator's cursor and changes live.

faq.question: How do I stop someone from overwriting my work?
faq.answer: Setting their access to Suggesting-only mode, or giving them Commenter permissions, prevents direct overwrites.

faq.question: Can I see who made a specific change?
faq.answer: Yes, Version History color-codes edits by contributor so you can trace exactly who changed what.

faq.question: Is there a limit to how many people can collaborate at once?
faq.answer: Google Docs supports a large number of simultaneous editors, though very high numbers may affect performance.
